children.name: Mohoua
children.description: A genus of small, active insectivores endemic to New Zealand, comprising three species with soft, predominantly brown and white plumage. They are highly social, often foraging in noisy, chattering flocks that move through the canopy in search of invertebrates. Two species are well known as hosts for the brood-parasitic Long-tailed Cuckoo and the Shining Cuckoo.

children.name: Mohoua albicilla
children.description: A small, active bird with a pure white head and underparts, brown back, and a lively, chattering call, endemic to the North Island of New Zealand. It lives in noisy, fast-moving flocks in the canopy of native forests, gleaning insects from leaves and bark. Its constant, cheerful calling makes it one of the most vocal inhabitants of North Island forests.

children.name: Mohoua ochrocephala
children.description: A bright yellow-headed bird with olive-yellow underparts, one of New Zealand's most colourful forest passerines, endemic to the South Island and Stewart Island. It was once common but has undergone a dramatic decline due to predation by introduced mammals and is now restricted to a few mainland forests and offshore islands. It is a primary host for the brood-parasitic Long-tailed Cuckoo.

children.name: Mohoua novaeseelandiae
children.description: A small, brown-and-white bird with a distinctive mewing call and a perpetually active manner, endemic to the South Island of New Zealand. Unlike the other two Mohoua species, it is primarily a bird of coastal scrub, forest edges, and subalpine shrubberies rather than tall forest. It is the smallest of the whiteheads and is a common host for the Shining Cuckoo.
